schools.summary: Paul turns to Abraham — the father of the nation, the supreme example of piety — to prove justification is by faith not works (Robertson, Barnes), asking what Abraham found 'as pertaining to the flesh' (by his own works), the answer being nothing to glory in before God (v.2).
Chrysostom and Theodoret: Paul confirms his doctrine from the patriarch the Jews revered, that the Jew cannot object.

themes: what shall we say that Abraham our father hath found
as pertaining to the flesh
the turn to Abraham
the decisive test of justification

schools.summary: If Abraham were justified by works, he would have ground for boasting (a claim to merit, Barnes, Robertson) — but not before God, where all boasting is silenced, justification by works being excluded (3:27).
Calvin: if Abraham was justified by embracing God's mercy by faith, he had nothing to glory in, bringing only a confession of his misery, a solicitation for mercy.

themes: if Abraham were justified by works, he hath whereof to glory
but not before God
the argument from boasting
no self-glory before God

schools.summary: The key text (Genesis 15:6): Abraham believed God (strong confidence in the promise, including the covenant of grace and Christ), and it was counted (elogisthe, reckoned, set on the credit side, Robertson) for righteousness; Barnes holds the act of believing was reckoned, faith the instrument not a transfer.

Calvin (with Ellicott): a forensic imputation — faith counted as righteousness because it lays hold on Christ, whose righteousness is the ground; not faith as a meritorious act but as the means of attaining the righteousness of God, which is therefore rightly called imputative.

debates.question: What was 'counted for righteousness' — the act of faith itself or Christ's righteousness laid hold of by faith?

sides.position: The act of believing was reckoned for righteousness — faith the instrument, not a transfer of another's righteousness
A forensic imputation — faith counted as righteousness because it lays hold on Christ, whose righteousness is the ground

themes: Abraham believed God, and it was counted unto him for righteousness
the key text on imputation
Genesis 15:6
faith reckoned for righteousness

schools.summary: To the worker (one who seeks to merit by works, claiming wages as his due) the reward (misthos, wages) is reckoned not of grace but of debt — earned and owed, claimable by right (Barnes, Robertson, Theodoret).

Calvin: if one merits by works, the reward is his due not grace; but faith receives righteousness as a free gift — the worker here is not one given to good works but one who seeks to merit something from God.

themes: to him that worketh is the reward reckoned of debt
not of grace, but of debt
the principle of works
grace and debt opposed

schools.summary: To him who works not but believes on him who justifies the ungodly, his faith is counted for righteousness — God justifying not the righteous but the impious, pardoning the sinner who believes; 'the whole Pauline gospel summed up in one word — God who justifies the ungodly' (Denney, Robertson, Barnes).

Calvin (with Wesley): none seeks the righteousness of faith but he who feels himself ungodly; God justifies by freely forgiving sinners — holiness does not precede justification, for to be made holy first would entirely set justification aside.

themes: to him that worketh not, but believeth on him that justifieth the ungodly
his faith is counted for righteousness
God justifies the ungodly
the heart of the gospel

schools.summary: Paul adds David (the most eminent king) as a second witness (Barnes, Robertson), citing Psalm 32 to show the blessedness of the man to whom God imputes righteousness without works — treating him as righteous by grace, not deeds.

Calvin: justification with Paul is the remission of sins; David describes blessedness as forgiveness, the works excluded entirely (not merely ceremonies) — righteousness is gratuitous, imputed without works.

themes: David describeth the blessedness of the man
unto whom God imputeth righteousness without works
David's confirming witness
righteousness imputed apart from works

schools.summary: David pronounces blessed (happy, highly favoured) those whose iniquities are forgiven (the debt remitted) and whose sins are covered (hidden from God's view, no more remembered, Barnes, Robertson) — the two-fold mercy of pardon, the same blessedness as imputed righteousness (v.6).

themes: Blessed are they whose iniquities are forgiven
whose sins are covered
the blessedness of forgiveness
pardon is justification

schools.summary: Blessed is the man to whom the Lord will not impute (reckon, charge) sin — the negative side of imputed righteousness, God not charging sin to his account (Barnes, Robertson), corresponding to the imputation of righteousness (v.6); the two sides of justification.

Calvin: David's subject is justification not sanctification (stopping short of 'in whose spirit is no guile'); the blessedness is the gratuitous non-imputation of sin, the debt wholly removed and no more remembered.

themes: Blessed is the man to whom the Lord will not impute sin
the non-imputation of sin
the negative side of imputed righteousness
gratuitous mercy

schools.summary: Paul asks whether the blessedness of imputed righteousness belongs to the circumcised (Jews) only or also to the uncircumcised (Gentiles), pressing the universality of justification by faith (Barnes, Robertson), and restates the key fact (v.3) — faith reckoned to Abraham — to set up the decisive argument (v.10).

themes: cometh this blessedness upon the circumcision only, or upon the uncircumcision also
faith was reckoned to Abraham for righteousness
the question of circumcision
the universality of justification by faith

schools.summary: Paul asks when Abraham's faith was reckoned and answers: not when circumcised but while still uncircumcised (Genesis 15 preceding Genesis 17 by years, Barnes, Robertson); Abraham justified by faith while still a Gentile, so justification does not depend on circumcision and is open to the uncircumcised.

themes: how was it reckoned? in circumcision or uncircumcision
Not in circumcision, but in uncircumcision
the decisive proof from timing
justification before circumcision

schools.summary: Circumcision was a sign and seal of the righteousness of faith Abraham already had while uncircumcised (not the cause but a confirming seal authenticating the prior gift, Barnes, Robertson), that he might be the father of all who believe, circumcised or not — the believing Gentiles his true children, righteousness imputed to them also.
Calvin (with Theodoret): circumcision sealed, did not produce, righteousness; Abraham's fatherhood extends to all who walk in the steps of his faith — the sign subordinate to the substance.

themes: a seal of the righteousness of the faith
father of all them that believe, though not circumcised
circumcision the seal of faith's righteousness
Abraham father of all believers

schools.summary: Abraham is the father of the circumcised — but not merely by the rite or physical descent; only of those who also walk in the steps of his faith (Barnes, Robertson), faith and not the flesh making the true child of Abraham even among the Jews.
Calvin (with Theodoret): circumcision alone does not make one Abraham's child; only faith does — the true children are those who imitate his faith.

themes: the father of circumcision to them who walk in the steps of that faith
not of the circumcision only
Abraham father of the believing circumcised
faith makes the true child

schools.summary: The promise that Abraham and his seed should be heir of the world (fulfilled in the universal blessing through Christ and the worldwide family of faith) came not through the law (not yet given) but through the righteousness of faith — by grace, received by believing (Barnes, Robertson).
Calvin (with Henry): the inheritance rests on the promise and faith, not the law, which came centuries after the promise — grounding the blessing in grace.

themes: the promise that he should be the heir of the world
not through the law, but through the righteousness of faith
the promise grounded in faith
the inheritance by grace not law

schools.summary: If the inheritance were by the law (law-keeping), faith would be emptied (made void) and the promise nullified (Barnes, Robertson) — law and promise mutually exclusive as grounds: if heirship is by law, faith is useless and the free promise destroyed.
Calvin: the law and faith cannot coexist as grounds of justification — to introduce law is to abolish the promise and faith; the inheritance must be by one or the other, not both.

themes: if they which are of the law be heirs, faith is made void
the promise made of none effect
the incompatibility of law and promise
law and grace mutually exclusive

schools.summary: The law, being violated by all, works wrath (condemnation and punishment, Barnes, Robertson) — it cannot give the inheritance but exposes guilt and brings the curse; and where no law is, there is no transgression (the law turning sin into formal violation of a known command).
Calvin (with Wesley): the law brings wrath because none keep it, so the promise must rest on grace not law — the law's office is to convict, not to confer the inheritance.

themes: the law worketh wrath
where no law is, there is no transgression
the law works wrath, not inheritance
the promise must rest on grace

schools.summary: The inheritance is of faith that it might be by grace (faith and grace going together, as law and debt do; the free promise requiring faith, Barnes, Robertson, Calvin), to the end the promise might be sure to all the seed — firm and certain to both Jews and Gentiles, Abraham the father of us all.

themes: it is of faith, that it might be by grace
the promise might be sure to all the seed
of faith by grace sure to all
Abraham the father of us all

schools.summary: Abraham was made the father of many nations (Genesis 17:5, fulfilled in the worldwide family of faith), believing God who quickens the dead (raising up seed from his own and Sarah's dead bodies, the God of resurrection, Barnes, Robertson) and calls things that be not as though they were (the Creator's power).
Calvin (with Henry): God calls into being what does not exist — the Creator's omnipotence; Abraham's faith rested on God's power to fulfill the promise against all natural impossibility.

themes: I have made thee a father of many nations
God, who quickeneth the dead, and calleth things which be not as though they were
Abraham's faith in the life-giving, creating God
God's omnipotence the ground of faith

schools.summary: Abraham, against all natural hope (his and Sarah's age making offspring impossible), believed in hope (resting on God's promise) — faith triumphing over natural impossibility (Barnes, Robertson) — that he might become the father of many nations, trusting 'so shall thy seed be' against the evidence of nature.
Calvin (with Theodoret): Abraham's faith rose above natural reason, resting wholly on the promise — believing in hope where there was no natural hope.

themes: who against hope believed in hope
that he might become the father of many nations
Abraham's faith against hope
faith triumphing over natural impossibility

schools.summary: Abraham was not weak in faith — his confidence not shaken (Barnes, Robertson) — though he considered his own body now dead (about a hundred) and the deadness of Sarah's womb; these impossibilities did not weaken his faith, weighing them honestly yet not overcome.
Calvin: faith does not ignore difficulties but rises above them, resting on God's power — Abraham not blind to the obstacles but undefeated by them.

themes: being not weak in faith
considered not his own body now dead, neither the deadness of Sarah's womb
Abraham's faith unweakened by impossibilities
faith rising above natural obstacles

schools.summary: Abraham staggered not at the promise through unbelief — not wavering or doubting (Barnes, Robertson) — but was strong (strengthened) in faith, giving glory to God.
Calvin (with Theodoret): faith glorifies God by honoring his power and truth — trusting his promise gives him the glory due his omnipotence and faithfulness, the deepest worship.

themes: he staggered not at the promise of God through unbelief
was strong in faith, giving glory to God
Abraham strong in faith
faith glorifies God

schools.summary: Abraham was fully persuaded (completely convinced, a settled entire conviction, not a wavering hope, Barnes, Robertson) that what God had promised he was able also to perform — resting on God's omnipotence.
Calvin (with Theodoret): the essence of faith — trusting not the visible probability but God's power and faithfulness to fulfill his word.

themes: being fully persuaded
what he had promised, he was able also to perform
Abraham's full persuasion of God's power
the settled assurance of faith

schools.summary: Because of this strong, God-glorifying faith (vv.18-21), it was imputed (reckoned) to Abraham for righteousness (Barnes, Gill) — restating the key text (v.3, Genesis 15:6), the conclusion of the description of his faith.
Calvin: it was this faith — trusting God's mercy and power — that was counted for righteousness, not as a work but as laying hold on God's promise.

themes: therefore it was imputed to him for righteousness
Abraham's faith imputed
the conclusion of the description
faith reckoned for righteousness

schools.summary: The record of Abraham's justification was written not for his sake alone (not merely history about him, Barnes, Robertson) but for the instruction of all believers — a pattern and assurance for all who would believe, a universal lesson.
Chrysostom (with Henry): God's dealings with Abraham are written for our learning and comfort, that we might know justification by faith is for us too.

themes: it was not written for his sake alone
that it was imputed to him
written for us also
Abraham's justification a universal lesson

schools.summary: Righteousness shall be imputed to us also, on the same terms as Abraham — if we believe on him who raised up Jesus our Lord from the dead (Barnes, Robertson), paralleling Abraham's faith in the God who quickens the dead (v.17); our faith and Abraham's have the same object, the life-giving God.
Calvin (with Cyril): as Abraham believed God's power to give life, so we believe in the God who raised Christ — the resurrection the ground of our justifying faith.

themes: for us also, to whom it shall be imputed
if we believe on him that raised up Jesus our Lord from the dead
the application to believers in the risen Christ
Abraham's faith and ours the same

schools.summary: Christ was delivered for our offences (to make atonement, satisfaction for our sins, Isaiah 53) and raised again for our justification — the death the proper cause of atonement, the resurrection its proof and seal (1 Corinthians 15:17, proving the atonement accepted and grounding our hope, Barnes, Ellicott).

Clarke: it is by Christ's death, not his obedience to the moral law, that we are saved — denying imputed active obedience; faith in his sacrificial death is reckoned for righteousness, the redemption through his blood.

Gill (with Calvin): Christ's righteousness, including his obedience and death, imputed to believers — delivered for our offences and raised for our justification, the whole work the ground of our acceptance.

debates.question: Is Christ's active obedience to the moral law imputed, or only the benefit of his death?

sides.position: Only the benefit of his sacrificial death is reckoned — we are saved by his death, not his obedience to the moral law
Christ's whole righteousness, obedience and death together, imputed to believers

themes: who was delivered for our offences
raised again for our justification
the two pillars of death and resurrection
the ground of justification in Christ
